text: There are a few things in a colorectal surgeon's operation that are more critical than the actual anastomosis. This is probably the portion of the operation that gives us the most anxiety and stress. One part of the operation that gives me personally significant anxiety is when staple lines on an EEA anastomosis overlap. This stems from a study that actually shows the relationship between the number of intersecting staple lines in anastomotic leakage. And this was published by Lee et al. out of Korea in which essentially looked at the number of intersecting staple lines and the incidence of an asthmatic leakage. And when you begin to increase the number of intersecting staple lines at one staple line, two intersecting staple lines, three intersecting staple lines, the chances of an asthmatic leakage actually went up.

The surgeon robotically mobilizes the small bowel out of the pelvis, prepares the rectum, and dissects the efferent limb of the descending colon, freeing it from the abdominal wall in a morbidly obese patient undergoing colostomy takedown.

The surgeon uses robotic scissors to sharply amputate tissue, employing the cut setting of cautery. Firefly imaging is used to assess perfusion, while the bedside assistant applies pressure to the abdominal wall.

The surgeon positions the VesselSealer Xtend at the inferior border below the skin within the peristomal hernia. The assistant has already placed the anvil through the colostomy and inserted a suture, enabling the surgeon to run a purse-string suture using the instrument.
The surgeon switches to a needle driver to perform a running purse-string suture, as the VesselSealer is not designed for needle handling.

The surgeon completes a running purse string suture around the anvil, which is placed intraluminally within the rectum. The rectum is dissected and prepared for sharp amputation. The EEA stapler is positioned transanally, upstream from the proctotomy site.
The surgeon begins running a purse string suture using a V-lock suture, starting from the outer edge and moving inward, then reversing the direction from inward to outward.

The surgeon extrudes the anvil spike completely from the lumen of the rectum, inverts the view to a thirty-degree upward angle using the X-eye, and completes the purse string suture on the posterior edge of the proctotomy. The taper is advanced further, resulting in the retrieval of two robust, healthy anastomotic doughnuts.
I hope this video was of some use. Thanks for taking the time to watch it. Take care.
